title: Private Link & Private Endpoints vs Service Endpoints
summary: Azure PaaS services (Storage, SQL, Key Vault) live on public endpoints by default. Private Link gives them a private IP *inside your VNet*, so your traffic to them never touches the public internet. Understand how that differs from the older Service Endpoints and you'll know why Private Link became the default for serious security.
analogy: A Private Endpoint is installing a private door from your office straight into the bank vault — you walk in without ever stepping onto the public street. A Service Endpoint is still using the bank's public front door, but the guard now recognizes your office badge and trusts you faster.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  VM[VM in subnet 10.0.1.4] --> PE[Private Endpoint 10.0.1.9 - a NIC in YOUR subnet]
  PE -->|Private Link over Microsoft backbone| PaaS[(Azure SQL / Storage / Key Vault)]
  DNS[Private DNS zone privatelink.*] -.resolves public name to 10.0.1.9.-> VM
  Internet((Public endpoint)) -. can be disabled .-x PaaS
body:
## First principles

When you create an Azure Storage account or SQL database, it gets a **public endpoint** — a name like `mystore.blob.core.windows.net` resolving to a public IP, reachable (subject to auth) from the internet. That's convenient, but it means your private VM talking to your own database sends traffic *out* to a public endpoint and back. Two problems follow: the data path touches the public internet, and the service is exposed to the world (protected only by keys and firewalls). The first-principles fix: bring the PaaS service *onto your private network* so the traffic never leaves it.

## Why it exists

Security teams hate that PaaS resources are internet-facing. Even with access keys and IP firewalls, a publicly reachable endpoint is an attack surface and a data-exfiltration path. The goal is **"my app should reach Storage over a private IP, and Storage should refuse the public internet entirely."** Azure built two mechanisms toward that goal, and they work very differently:

1. **Service Endpoints** (the older, lighter approach).
2. **Private Link / Private Endpoints** (the newer, stronger approach — now the recommended default).

Knowing why the second largely replaced the first is the heart of this lesson.

## How it works

**Service Endpoints.** You enable a service endpoint on a *subnet* for a service type (e.g. `Microsoft.Storage`). This does two things: it gives traffic from that subnet an **optimized route** to the service over Microsoft's backbone (not the public internet), and it lets the service's firewall accept that subnet by **VNet identity** rather than by IP. But — crucially — the service **keeps its public endpoint and public IP**. You're still talking *to* the public resource; you've just made the path better and the firewall smarter. Service endpoints are free, simple, but don't extend to on-prem and don't give the resource a private address.

**Private Link / Private Endpoints.** This is categorically different. A **Private Endpoint** is an actual **network interface with a private IP from your subnet** that maps to a specific PaaS resource. Your `mystore` storage account now answers at `10.0.1.9` *inside your VNet*. Traffic flows over **Azure Private Link** across Microsoft's backbone directly to that one resource. Now you can:

- **Disable the public endpoint entirely** — the resource becomes unreachable from the internet, full stop.
- **Reach it from on-prem** through your VPN/ExpressRoute, because it's just a private IP in your VNet.
- **Map to one specific resource instance**, not a whole service class — tighter blast radius.

**The DNS piece is essential.** Your app still uses the public name `mystore.blob.core.windows.net`. For that name to resolve to the private IP, Azure uses a **Private DNS zone** (e.g. `privatelink.blob.core.windows.net`) linked to your VNet. Inside the VNet the name now resolves to `10.0.1.9`; outside it still resolves publicly. If you create a Private Endpoint but **forget to wire up the private DNS zone**, your app keeps resolving the public IP and the whole exercise silently fails — this is the single most common Private Link mistake.

## In practice

```bash
# Create a Private Endpoint into your subnet for a specific Storage account.
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group <rg> --name <pe-name> \
  --vnet-name <vnet> --subnet <subnet> \
  --private-connection-resource-id <storage-account-id> \
  --group-id blob --connection-name <conn>

# Wire DNS so the public name resolves to the private IP inside the VNet.
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group <rg> --name privatelink.blob.core.windows.net
az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group <rg> --zone-name privatelink.blob.core.windows.net \
  --name dnslink --virtual-network <vnet> --registration-enabled false
# (then create the A record / DNS zone group binding the PE to the zone)
```

Finish by setting the storage account's public network access to **Disabled** — now the only way in is your private endpoint.

## When to use which

- **Private Endpoint** when you want the resource off the public internet, need on-prem access to it, or have compliance/exfiltration concerns. This is the modern default for sensitive PaaS.
- **Service Endpoint** when you just want a fast, free way to let a subnet reach a service and lock the service firewall to your VNet, and you're fine with the public endpoint remaining. Lighter, but weaker.

## Gotchas

- **Forgetting Private DNS = silent failure.** The #1 mistake. Without the linked `privatelink.*` zone and its records, the app resolves the public IP and bypasses your private endpoint.
- **Service Endpoints don't remove the public endpoint.** The resource is still internet-facing; you've optimized the route and firewall identity, not privatized the resource.
- **Service Endpoints don't reach on-prem.** They apply to traffic *from the VNet subnet*; your datacenter can't use them. Private Endpoints can be reached from on-prem over VPN/ExpressRoute.
- **Private Endpoints cost money and consume an IP.** Each is a billed resource using a private IP per linked resource — plan address space and budget for many of them.
- **Lock down after you privatize.** A private endpoint is only half the win; also set the resource's public network access to Disabled, or the public door stays open.
- **One PE targets one resource (sub-resource).** Storage blob vs file vs table need separate `group-id` endpoints — they're not one-size-fits-all.
teachItBack: Azure PaaS services default to public endpoints, which means private-to-private traffic can still ride the internet and the resource stays internet-exposed. Service Endpoints partly fix this by giving a subnet an optimized backbone route and letting the service firewall trust the VNet — but the public endpoint remains and on-prem can't use it. Private Link goes further: a Private Endpoint is a real NIC with a private IP in your subnet mapped to one specific resource, letting you disable the public endpoint entirely and reach the resource privately, even from on-prem. The catch is DNS — you must link the privatelink.* private zone so the public name resolves to the private IP, or the whole thing silently falls back to public.

glossary.definition: The Azure technology that delivers a PaaS (or partner) service privately into your VNet over Microsoft's backbone.
A network interface with a private IP in your subnet that maps to a specific PaaS resource, making it reachable privately.
A subnet setting that optimizes the route to a PaaS service and lets its firewall trust the VNet, while the service keeps its public endpoint.
A Private DNS zone (e.g. privatelink.blob.core.windows.net) that resolves a service's public name to its private endpoint IP inside the VNet.
A resource setting that turns off the public endpoint, leaving the private endpoint as the only way in.
